coredata.dc:title: The colorimetric identification chips of artificial visual recognition and rapid digital response for detecting pollutants
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Shichao
Wang, Li
Hu, Lin
Tao, Qiangqiang
Hu, Qiuyan
Feng, Fu
Jin, Wei
Li, Chuanbin
Hu, Ning
coredata.dc:description: Rapid detection technology has garnered significant interest and been widely applied due to its advantages of rapidity, accuracy, stability, and simplicity. A smart digital detection platform for water pollutants based on CI-Chips (colorimetric identification chips) is presented. The designed platform is utilized to capture and identify color data, and to establishes the quantitative relationship between color information and pollutant concentration. Simultaneously, the CI-chips fabricated via embossing technology enable visual and quantitative analysis of pollutants by using this platform. The introduction of smart devices enabled us to realize the concept of digitization and break through the limitations imposed by complex instruments. Through this detection platform, rapid detection of six kinds of metal ions can be achieved simultaneously. The maximum detectable concentration of pollutants can attain 3500 μg/L, with the limits of detection (LOD) ranging from 70 μg/L to 130 μg/L. The calibration graphs exhibited excellent linearity, as evidenced by the coefficients of determination exceeding 0.995. The CI-Chips have the advantages of portability and practicability, cost-effectiveness and so on. As an environmentally friendly production, it will be widely utilized in environmental monitoring and assay in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Portable rapid detection system
CI-Chips
Digital imaging
Ionic contaminants

CI-detection with quantum dots based on fluorescence
Responses of CI-Chips to pollutant concentration

We are grateful for the assistance from the National Natural Science Foundation of China Youth Program (Fund number: 52304428).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Digital imaging inspection system workflow chart. (a) Diagram of the microstructure of the colorimetric identification chips.

The effect of reaction time on color intensity of reaction systems. Cr (Ⅵ) system with chromogenic reagent volumes of 30 μl (a), 40 μl (b) and 50 μl (c). Fe (Ⅱ) system with volumes of 30 μl (d), 40 μl (e) and 50 μl (f). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Effect of the amount of chromogenic reagent on the color intensity of the reaction system. (a, b, d, e) are the changes of color intensity of Cr (Ⅵ) and Fe (Ⅱ) system with the amount of chromogenic reagent. (c, f) are the 3D response surface curves and contour plots of Cr (Ⅵ) and Fe (Ⅱ). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs in different buffer liquid systems. (b) Comparison of fluorescence intensity of RCDs in different buffer liquid systems. (c) Fluorescence intensity change of 0.1 mM Hg (Ⅱ) and R-CDs with reaction time. (d) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs after adding 13 common metal ions. (e) Radial bar chart of quenching efficiency of R-CDs by metal ions. (f) The anti-interference ability of R-CDs to Hg (Ⅱ) detection.

Color reaction standard curve. (a) Cr6+ color reaction standard curve. (b) Fe (Ⅱ) color reaction standard curve. (c) Zn (Ⅱ) color reaction standard curve. (d) Cu (Ⅱ) color reaction standard curve. (e) Mn (Ⅳ) color reaction standard curve. (f) Linear fitting of R-CDs for Hg (Ⅱ) detection. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Determination of pollutants in natural water.
Comparison of common pollutant detection methods.

row.entry: low detection limit, high sensitivity, good selectivity,
high sample pre-treatment requirements, complex instrumentation, high cost
(Venkatesan et al., 2020; Zhu, A. et al., 2023)
simple operation, high accuracy, good repeatability
fast speed, high sensitivity, good accuracy
The reagents are harmful to humans and the environment; the instruments are expensive and slow to detect.
Low detection limit, high sensitivity and resolution, simultaneous determination of several elements
Complex operation, complicated material synthesis, long testing time
short analysis time, simple operation, low professional requirements and high sensitivity.
Susceptible to environmental influences and photobleaching.
Simple operation, low cost, fast analysis and visual reading.
Low sensitivity, poor stability and large quantitative error.

head.ce:title: The colorimetric identification chips of artificial visual recognition and rapid digital response for detecting pollutants

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Resources and Environmental Engineering
Wuhan University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid detection technology has garnered significant interest and been widely applied due to its advantages of rapidity, accuracy, stability, and simplicity. A smart digital detection platform for water pollutants based on CI-Chips (colorimetric identification chips) is presented. The designed platform is utilized to capture and identify color data, and to establishes the quantitative relationship between color information and pollutant concentration. Simultaneously, the CI-chips fabricated via embossing technology enable visual and quantitative analysis of pollutants by using this platform. The introduction of smart devices enabled us to realize the concept of digitization and break through the limitations imposed by complex instruments. Through this detection platform, rapid detection of six kinds of metal ions can be achieved simultaneously. The maximum detectable concentration of pollutants can attain 3500 μg/L, with the limits of detection (LOD) ranging from 70 μg/L to 130 μg/L. The calibration graphs exhibited excellent linearity, as evidenced by the coefficients of determination exceeding 0.995. The CI-Chips have the advantages of portability and practicability, cost-effectiveness and so on. As an environmentally friendly production, it will be widely utilized in environmental monitoring and assay in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pollutants in water environment mainly originate from industrial effluents, mining wastewater, sewage irrigation, the use of metal products, and other human activities (Mukhopadhyay et al., 2022; Shamim et al., 2022). Among them, heavy metals, such as copper, zinc, chromium, are highly toxic and persistent pollutants that have a severe impact on the environment (He et al., 2025). The copper concentration in untreated electroplating wastewater can reach as high as 1000–5000 μg/L. Moreover, the average concentration of lead, cadmium, zinc and copper in soil surpass the prescribed level by 1.5 %, 7.0 %, 0.9 % and 2.1 %, respectively (Wang, Y. et al., 2024). Crucially, these contaminants are non-biodegradable in water and will combine with other toxins to form more hazardous organic complexes. They can cause poisoning, carcinogenic, teratogenic and mutagenic hazards to human health, and can affect the growth, reproduction and physiological functions of aquatic organisms (Bahrami et al., 2022; Bai et al., 2023; Oladimeji et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2021). Hence, accurate, sensitive and efficient detection of pollutants in water can provide an essential guarantee for environmental protection and human health (Zhu, J. et al., 2023).

At present, the commonly employed metal ion detection methods include inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, atomic absorption spectrometry, electrochemical sensors, and X-ray fluorescence spectrometry (Chen et al., 2022; Wang, S. et al., 2023; Yuan et al., 2024). These detection methods possess the advantages of low detection limits (Zhu, J. et al., 2023), high sensitivity and selectivity, and accurate analytical results. The disadvantages (Chai et al., 2010; Huang et al., 2022) lie in the complicated sample preparation, the requirement for precise and costly instruments and equipment, slow speed of detection and analysis, lengthy testing cycle, complexity of pretreatment procedures and analytical techniques (Ou et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2019), and high demands on the professionalism of operators, which render them unsuitable for on-site detection (Najeeb et al., 2018; Pouyanfar et al., 2022). Compared with other methods, optical methods have the advantages of high selectivity and sensitivity, low cost, and simple operation (Wang et al., 2021), Consequently, it is possible to establish a rapid, stable and simple method for the simultaneous determination of multiple metal pollutants within water systems.

Rapid detection technology constitutes one of the most promising research directions (Qi et al., 2024; Wang, S. et al., 2024), enabling the rapid detection of the concentration of water pollutants without the utilization of additional instruments (Jin et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2021). Smartphone-based digital imaging colorimetry (Geng et al., 2023; Wang, H. et al., 2023) (SDIC) represents an innovative approach for the detection of pollutant ions within aqueous media. The detection system divides the image into three color channels (Caleb and Alshana, 2021; Caleb et al., 2023): red, green and blue, followed by the construction of a color quantization model to reflect the relationship between image data and pollutant concentration. Subsequently, the acquired results are analyzed by the digital detection platform, ultimately realizing the analysis and measurement of the pollutant content within the actual sample (Fan et al., 2021; Ren et al., 2022; Zheng et al., 2022). Compared with traditional detection methods, paper-based detection chips possess the advantages of convenient storage and portability, simple operation and low cost (Xu et al., 2025), and are capable of becoming a new method for on-site application with the assistance of smartphone. Consequently, the digital detection platform is a detection method that combines paper-based chips with intelligent equipment (Geng et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2022). It exhibits attributes such as sensitivity, rapidity, portability and the capacity for visual detection assessment (Capitán-Vallvey et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2022), rendering it suitable for rapid on-site detection.

Herein, a digital detection platform for water pollutants based on visual recognition and SDIC was presented. The designed program was utilized to capture and identify color data, and to establish a quantitative relationship between color information and pollutant concentration. The introduction of smart devices enabled the realization of the concept of digitization and the breaking through of the limitations imposed by complex instruments, endowing the platform with the advantages of yielding accurate test results, featuring low detection limits, and allowing for data recording. At the same time, CI-chips tailored for the detection platform were fabricated, which had the advantages of simple operation, fast detection speed, cost-effectiveness, and portability. These attributes rendered the production suitable for water detection in surface water, groundwater and wastewater, as well as remote areas. Moreover, it could be employed to conduct rapid testing of water pollutants in areas lacking specialized equipment or in areas where special events take place, thereby ensuring people's water safety. So the product has a good application prospect in the fields of pollution detection and environmental monitoring.

Analytical-grade reagents were used throughout the study unless otherwise specified. The solutions containing Cr (Ⅵ), Fe (Ⅱ), Zn (Ⅱ), Cu (Ⅱ), Mn (Ⅳ), Hg (Ⅱ) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). These reagents were utilized to provide contaminant solutions with standard concentration gradients. Chromogenic reagents: Diphenyl carbamide, 1,10-Phenanthroline, dithizone, Sodium diethyldithiocarbamate, formaldehyde oxime were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). These reagents were used to prepare the chromogenic layer of the CI-chips and to optimize the color reaction. Citric acid, Carbamide, N,N-Dimethylformamide were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Additionally, H2SO4, H3PO4, HO-NH3Cl, Na2S2O3, CH3COONH4, C3H6O, CH3COONa, CH3COOH were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). These reagents were used in the buffer layer to adjust the pH of the system and remove interference to maintain the stability of the color development system. Chromatography paper was purchased from Shanghai Bestest Biological Technology Co., Ltd (Co., Ltd., China). The wax blocks were purchased from Shenzhen Times Qituo Technology (Co., Ltd, China.).

The experimental apparatus consisted of a vivo X100 smartphone (manufactured by vivo Technologies Co., Ltd., China), Computer PS color extraction software, and a laboratory-fabricated colorimetric box. Photographs were acquired using the vivo X100 smartphone, and the RGB values of the images were analyzed with the Computer PS color extraction software. Freeze-dryer purchased from Beijing Biocool Experimental Instrument Co., Ltd (Co., Ltd, China.). Centrifuge purchased from Hunan Xiangyi Laboratory Instrument Development Co., Ltd (Co., Ltd, China.).

The preparation of the CI-chips necessitated the testing and optimizing the buffer reagents, chromogenic reagents, and reaction time of the system, to obtain the optimal colorimetric system for different pollutants. The selected chromogenic reagents were chosen in accordance with the National Environmental Protection Standards of the People's Republic of China (China Environmental Science Press). For the convenience of operation and recording, separate optimization experiments were conducted for each pollutant to be detected. In order to test various suitable reaction conditions, a series of gradient solutions with standard concentrations were prepared for the pollutants to be tested. Subsequently, different types of buffer reagents and different doses of chromogenic reagents (0–3.5 mg/L) were successively added. Under the condition of different color developing times (3–15 min), color readings were obtained using the digital detection platform. Subsequently, the color intensities of each experimental group were compared to select the most appropriate system.

Carbon dots, as fluorescent nanomaterials, possess excellent water solubility, low toxicity, chemical and photostability (Liu et al., 2019), and are regarded as promising novel materials for the development of new biosensors and environmental monitoring apparatuses (Jia et al., 2021). Consequently, red fluorescent carbon dots (R-CDs) were used to detect the concentration of Hg (Ⅱ). The solvent-thermal method was adopted for the synthesis of R-CDs for Hg (Ⅱ) detection. The detailed procedures were as follows: Citric acid and urea were dissolved in 10 ml of N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) via ultrasonic treatment. Subsequently, the solution was transferred into a 30 ml polytetrafluoroethylene hydrothermal reactor, and a reaction was carried out at 180 °C for 6 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the supernatant was removed, and the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. Then, 20 ml of 0.05 g/ml NaOH aqueous solution was added, and the mixture was centrifuged at 12000 r/min for 15 min, followed by the collection of the precipitate. Finally, the precipitate was dialyzed for 36 h and subsequently freeze-dried to obtain the solid powder of R-CDs.

The rapid detection platform was utilized for the acquisition of standard data. The calibration was performed using inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry. Based on the determined optimal colorimetric conditions, reaction time and chromogenic reagent, standard curve measurement experiments were conducted, followed by the preparation of CI-chips.

As depicted in Fig. 1 (a), the CI-chips were composed of four components: the top color sampling layer, the buffer solution layer, the chromogenic layer and the bottom protection layer. The chromogenic layer employed chromatography paper as the base material, and the chip was designed to form hydrophilic and hydrophobic zones using CAD drawing. The hydrophobic area was coated with wax blocks that could penetrate the paper to establish a hydrophobic barrier and preclude the solution from leaking out. Meanwhile, it could create an isolation among multiple detection units to prevent the solutions to be tested from influencing each other and introducing errors into the detection results. The hydrophilic area comprised a buffer layer and a chromogenic layer, onto which the required reagents were embossed onto the corresponding color-development zone by means of the embossing technique. The top and bottom protective layers were smeared with wax blocks to prevent the solution from leaking out and to reduce the background value of the detection chip.

The principle of digital imaging colorimetry is to establish the quantitative relationship between the colorimetric value and the concentration of the analyte to be measured based on the color parameters acquired by smart digital equipment (Liu et al., 2018). The architecture of the digital imaging colorimetric platform was as follows: LED lights were embedded inside the chromaticity monitoring box to provide illumination. A smartphone was fixed on the outside of the chromaticity monitoring box, and color acquisition software was used to obtain the color value of the sample to be measured through the camera of the mobile phone (Caleb and Alshana, 2021). The image was read by a smartphone recognition program and the numerical values were converted to RGB color values. To obtain accurate data, each data image was measured five times in the specified area to obtain the RGB average value.

Matter exhibits different colors due to the absorption of certain wavelengths of light, and the colors perceived by us are the complementary colors of those absorbed. The Photoshop software read the corresponding RGB color values of the solution and selected the appropriate data model as the response value of color intensity for the optimization of the experimental conditions. The color was calculated using the following formula:
(1)
I
X
total
2
2
=
I R 2
+
I G 2
+
I B 2
2
Where IR, IG, and IB are the color intensity test values of red, green and blue, respectively, IX total is total color intensity.

According to Lambert-Beer Law, the initial RGB color values were converted into logarithmic ratios (Chen et al., 2022; Firdaus et al., 2014), the standard curve was plotted by collecting the R, G, and B color values from the colorimetric reactions of various concentrations of pollutants. The color conversion formulas were as follows:
(2)
I R
= log
(
R 0
/
R S
)
(3)
I G
= log
(
G 0
/
G S
)
(4)
I B
= log
(
B 0
/
B S
)
Where IR, IG, and IB are the effective intensity of red, green and blue, respectively, R0, G0, B0 and RS, GS, BS are the red, green and blue values of blank and sample.

Response surface analysis (RSA) is a statistical tool for multivariate optimization, which can investigate the effects of interactions between various factors on non-independent variables and obtain the relationships between each factor and the response value. It can reduce the number of experiments, shorten the experimental time and cycle, and improve the accuracy. It is widely applied in fields such as food safety and environmental monitoring, serving as a feasible method to reduce workload in environmental engineering. Response surface analysis was used to obtain the optimal relationship between the time of the experiment and the chromogenic agent used, so as to better prepare the subsequent CI-chips.

The smartphone-based detection software provided to users mainly comprises three major aspects as illustrated in Fig. 2 (Geng et al., 2023; Khalaf et al., 2023; Zheng et al., 2022): mode selection, detection and calibration, and data management. The mode selection entails the selection of the target contaminant and algorithm, enabling users to make choices between different pollutants and adjust the built-in parameters, thereby facilitating precise and convenient computation. The detection and calibration segment encompasses curve measurement calculation and detection numerical computation. Users can directly import standard curves and data processing methods. During field detection, users are enabled to calculate based on the measured experimental data and utilize the curve to determine the concentration of pollutants to be measured. Finally, the data management is characterized by the preservation of detection images and remote data sharing. The software automatically saves images for subsequent analysis.

The user detection process consists of the following steps: ① Mode selection: Select the type and detection mode of pollutants to be measured as required. ② Image acquisition: Employ the device's camera to obtain images of water samples or permit users to select images from the phone's system files. ③ Color extraction: Automatically identify specific color regions within the image, and enable users to select areas of interest via touchscreen interaction. ④ Concentration calculation: The system automatically calculates the corresponding pollutant concentration in the tested water sample based on the identified RGB color values and the standard curve data set within the system. ⑤ Result presentation: Display and save the recognized color, RGB values, pollutant type and concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fast detection with high sensitivity and accuracy on the CI-Chips
ce:section.ce:para: CI-Chips represented an all-in-one tool facilitating rapid multi-target detection. Accordingly, the optimal reaction time of each component was separately discussed. The color intensity of Cr (Ⅵ) within 3–15 min reaction systems were measured as shown in Fig. 3 (a)–(c). The experimental results demonstrated that the color intensity of the reaction exhibited minimal variation with time when the concentration of chromogenic reagent was constant. This was attributed to the fact that the color reaction of Cr (Ⅵ) was instantaneously completed and the color was stable. Extending the duration of the reaction could eliminate possible interference.

The color intensity changes of Fe (Ⅱ) within 3–15 min were determined. In the initial stage, the potential interference ions could affect the color intensity of the system. As the reaction continued, the interference gradually diminished. According to Fig. 3(d)–(f), when the reaction time reached 9 min, the color intensity of the reaction was relatively high and the change was stable. The addition of ammonium acetate in the buffer layer could stabilize the pH of solution and eliminate the interference of metal ions, thereby enhancing the accuracy and reliability of the experiment.

The color intensity changes of the system during the reaction time of 3–15 min were compared, as illustrated in Figs. S1(a)–(c). The color intensity of the Zn (Ⅱ) system exhibited significant changes with varying amounts of chromogenic reagent. Meanwhile, sodium acetate was added to the buffer layer, to control the pH range of the solution within 4.0–5.5, enabling zinc ions to form stable chelates with dithizone.

By comparing the color intensity changes of the system over the period of 3–15 min, respectively, the color intensity changes were shown in Figs. S1(d)–(f). The results demonstrated that Cu (Ⅱ) could react with DDTC rapidly. The color intensity of the system exhibited minimal variation with the color development time, and the color intensity distribution was more uniform. During the experiment, an ammonium chloride-ammonium hydroxide buffer layer was used. Its function was to maintain the reaction system within a specific pH range and ensure the smooth progress of the complexation reaction between DDTC and Cu (Ⅱ).

By comparing the color intensity changes at 3 min, 6 min, 9 min, 12 min and 15 min, the color intensity changes of the system were depicted in Figs. S1(g)–(i). The color reaction of Mn (Ⅳ) was very rapid, and the color intensity of Mn (Ⅳ) system exhibited minimal variation with different times. Ammonium-hydroxylamine hydrochloride buffer layer could adjust the pH value of the solution, and hydroxylamine hydrochloride had a reducing effect, which could reduce the high manganese ions in water to tetravalent manganese ions, making it more suitable for color reaction. The color reaction of interfering ions could be eliminated simultaneously by prolonging the color development time appropriately.

For the test of the amount of chromogenic reagent in the Cr (Ⅵ) color reaction, the color intensity was measured at 6 min and 9 min, as illustrated in Fig. 4 (a)and(b). It was found that the amount of diphenylcarbamide had a negligible influence on color intensity. A 3D response surface was used to analyze the influence of time and chromogenic agent on color intensity, as shown in Fig. 4(c). The results showed that the amount of chromogenic reagent had a significant influence on color intensity. Considering the relatively high content of Cr (Ⅵ) in practical applications, the additional amount of 40 μl/ml chromogenic reagent was selected, and the reaction time was set at 9 min.

To determine the amount of reagent for the Fe (Ⅱ) reaction, the color intensity curves with different amounts of chromogenic reagent were plotted at 6 min and 9 min respectively, as shown in Fig. 4(d)and(e). The system with 40 μl color developer dosage at 6min exhibited the best color development effect, and the color change was obvious. Analysis was carried out using a 3D response surface, as shown in Fig. 4(f). The results demonstrated that the amount of the chromogenic agent had a significant impact on the color intensity of the system. In this experiment, the amount of the chromogenic agent corresponding to the highest color intensity was selected for the subsequent experiments. Therefore, the indicator dosage of 40 μl/ml was finally selected for the subsequent experiments, and the reaction time was set as 9 min for stability.

Determination of the dosage of chromogenic reagent in Zn (Ⅱ) reaction experiments was carried out when the chromogenic time was 9 min and 12 min, as shown in Figs. S2(a)–(b). When the amount of chromogenic reagent was 60 μl, the color intensity of the reaction was significantly different. When the amount of reagent was more than 100 μl, the color of reagent itself had a great influence on the color of the reaction system, and the reagent precipitated when the reaction was incomplete. Based on the response surface analysis in Figs. S2(c) and 60 μl/ml chromogenic reagent was selected for subsequent experiments, and the subsequent reaction time was determined to be 9 min.

To determine the amount of DDTC for the Cu (Ⅱ) reaction, the color intensity curves with different amounts of chromogenic reagent were plotted at 6 min and 9 min, respectively, as shown in Figs. S2(d)–(e). When the amount of chromogenic reagent reaches 100 μl, the color intensity changed steadily. 3D response surface was used to analyze the influence of color developing time and chromogenic reagent on the color intensity of the system, as shown in Fig. S2(f). When the dosage of chromogenic reagent was set at 100 μl/ml for subsequent experiments, and the subsequent reaction time was 9 min, the color rendering effect of the system was optimal.

The experiment for determining the dosage of Mn (Ⅳ) chromogenic reagent was conducted at 6 min and 9 min as shown in Figs. S2(g)–(h). When the amount of chromogenic reagent ranged from 15 μl/ml to 35 μl/ml, the color intensity of the reaction system exhibited minimal variation. However, the color intensity was the lowest when the amount of chromogenic reagent was 20 μl/ml, and the color reaction effect was the best. Meanwhile, the response surface was employed to analyze the influence of time and the amount of chromogenic reagent on the reactive system, and the results were shown in Fig. S2(i). The results demonstrated that the influence of the amount of chromogenic reagent on the color system was less than that of time. Consequently, the subsequent reaction time was determined to be 9 min, and 20 μl/ml chromogenic reagent was selected for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CI-detection with quantum dots based on fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: For the purpose of enhancing CI-detection with rapidity and selectivity, quantum dots were employed. Buffer solution system optimization: The 200 ml DMF solution of R-CDs was diluted to 2 mL using deionized water with pH of 7.0, PBS buffer solution, HEPES buffer solution and citrate buffer solution, respectively. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 550 nm. As illustrated in Fig. 5 (a) and (b), the luminescence of R-CDs was the strongest in PBS buffer, followed by citrate buffer, and the weakest in deionized water. Hence, PBS buffer is selected as the best buffer system for subsequent tests in this study.

Optimization of the reaction time was performed: Fig. 5(c) depicted the change in fluorescence intensity with reaction time during the reaction between R-CDs and Hg (Ⅱ) It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of the probe was instantaneously quenched after incubating R-CDs with metal ions, and the fluorescence intensity decreased rapidly within 1 min. The fluorescence intensity of Hg (Ⅱ) sensing system was stable at approximately 3 min after incubation. Therefore, the reaction time between R-CDs and Hg (Ⅱ) was set at 3 min.

Selective and competitive analysis was performed: The fluorescence responses of R-CDs to 12 common metal ions were recorded using a steady-state fluorescence spectrometer. As shown in Fig. 5(d), the fluorescence of R-CDs was quenched when Hg (Ⅱ) was added, and the fluorescence quenching efficiency (ΔF = F0 - F) was 78.06 %, while the quenching efficiency of the other 10 metal ions was relatively low, ranging from 4.9 % to 13.68 % (Fig. 5(e)). This indicated that the R-CDs fluorescent probe had strong selectivity for Hg (Ⅱ) and possessed the potential to detect Hg (Ⅱ).

To evaluate the specificity of R-CDs towards Hg (Ⅱ), the effect of potential competitive ions on the fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs sensing system was measured under experimental conditions. Hg (Ⅱ) was added to the R-CDs solution system containing different metal ions. As shown in Fig. 5(f), the fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs showed no significant alteration except in the presence of Hg (Ⅱ). The results of selective and competitive tests demonstrated that R-CDs fluorescent probe could accurately determine Hg (Ⅱ) under complex conditions and had anti-interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Responses of CI-Chips to pollutant concentration
ce:section.ce:para: The reaction principle of the DPCI method for the determination of Cr (Ⅵ) is that in an acidic solution, Cr (Ⅵ) reacts with diphenyl carbamide to form a purplish-red compound, and the color of this compound is positively correlated with the content of Cr (Ⅵ). The results show that the linear regression equation is y = 0.26873C(mg/L)-0.02359, R2 = 0.990.

The principle of the 1,10-phenanthroline method for the determination of Fe (Ⅱ) content is that Fe (Ⅱ) forms a stable orange-red complex with nitrogen group in a solution within the pH range of 3–9. The linear relationship between Fe (Ⅱ) and color intensity is found in the range of 0.3–2.1 mg/L under selected experimental conditions. The linear regression equation of B channel is y = 0.14090C (mg/L)-0.00653, R2 = 0.998 (Fig. 6 (b)).

The principle of the dithizone method for detecting Zn (Ⅱ) content is that Zn (Ⅱ) interacts with thiocarbamyl groups to form red chelate in an acetate buffer medium with pH 4.0–5.5. The relationship between Zn (Ⅱ) and color intensity is linear in the range of 0.5–3.5 mg/L under selected experimental conditions. The linear regression equation of B channel is y = 0.01446C (mg/L) +0.01587, R2 = 0.998 (Fig. 6(c)).

The principle of the DDTC method for detecting Cu (Ⅱ) content is that Cu (Ⅱ) reacts with thiocarbamate groups in an ammoniacal solution (pH 9–10) to form a yellow-brown complex with a molar ratio of 1: 2. Under the selected experimental conditions, Cu (Ⅱ) exhibits a linear relationship with color intensity in the range of 0.2–1.4 mg/L, and the linear regression equation of B channel is y = 0.08358C (mg/L)-0.01026, R2 = 0. 999 (Fig. 6(d)).

The reaction principle of the formaldehyde oxime colorimetric method for detecting Mn (Ⅳ): Mn (Ⅳ) is oxidized by dissolved oxygen in an alkaline solution with pH 9.0–10.0 to form Mn (Ⅳ), and generates a brown complex with carbamyl groups. The relationship between Mn (Ⅳ) and color intensity is linear in the range of 0.5–3.5 mg/L under selected experimental conditions. The linear regression equation of G channel is y = 0.10491C (mg/L)-0.02630, R2 = 0. 998 (Fig. 6(e)).

The sensitivity detection of R-CDs towards Hg (Ⅱ) is shown in Fig. 6(f). It can be seen from the figure that the pink color of the solution gradually becomes lighter with the concentration of Hg (Ⅱ) under natural light. The red fluorescence is also gradually quenched under UV irradiation, indicating that R-CDs can make a two-mode colorimetric response to Hg (Ⅱ) under both natural and UV irradiation. The detection of Hg (Ⅱ) by R-CDs shows a good linear relationship, R2 = 0. 995 and the detection limit is 16.2 nM.

The response of the CI-chips to pollutants can be imported into the detection platform and saved. During the detection work, the water sample to be tested generates a color response on the hydrophilic layer of the chips. By utilizing the digital rapid detection platform of smartphones, the color values can be quickly captured and the detection results can be calculated to obtain the pollutant concentration values. Smart devices can accurately capture tiny color changes and conduct rapid calculations, so the detection results are efficient and accurate.

To verify the practicability of the detection platform, the method was applied to the detection of water samples from East Lake (Wuchang District, Wuhan City, Hubei Province). Each index was analyzed in 5 parallel experiments, and the results were shown in Table 1 . Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry was employed to compare the accuracy, and the relative standard deviation was within the range of 0.54 %–1.07 %. The analysis results demonstrated that the CI-chips based on visual recognition could reliably and effectively detect the heavy metal content in the actual water bodies, and had the potential for practical application.

In recent years, the commonly used detection methods of water pollutants are presented in Table 2 . Compared with other detection methods, the digital rapid detection platform have the following characteristics: (1) Leveraging visual recognition colorimetry for color identification processing, it enables the precise detection of low-concentration pollutants; (2) Non-destructive detection: This method precludes environmental disruption by obviating the necessity for complicated operational procedures; (3) Portability and practicality: The digital imaging colorimetry equipment is progressively evolving to a more refined state. The portable detection system is facile to operate and is suitable for water quality detection in wild and remote areas; (4) Economic efficiency: Compared with large-scale detection instruments and equipment, digital imaging colorimetry boasts low equipment costs and maintenance expenses, which is beneficial to large-scale applications.

The integration of digital detection platform and CI-chips makes this method the fastest, most environmentally friendly, and most cost-effective portable detection method, playing an important role in the field of high-throughput detection of heavy metal ions in water. Nevertheless, a substantial detection error is incurred when the application is employed for detection in water with a low pollutant concentration. Hence, this study ought to be directed towards attaining higher detection accuracy to mitigate the detection error resulting from low pollutant concentration. At the same time, future research will be carried out in the direction of increasing the diversity of pollution, enhancing detection accuracy and speed, reducing the costs, and expanding the scope of application. In addition, a new type of sensor based on CI-chips can be designed, so that the measurement of water pollution concentration can be carried out at different water depths, which is convenient for real-time monitoring of water environmental changes.

The detection platform is designed for water pollutants based on artificial visual recognition and rapid digital integrated technology. The tools enable the integrated detection of 6 pollutants in combination with smartphones. The detection process can be completed within 5–10 min, with a detection range of 0–3 mg/L, and a lowest detection limit of 0.07 mg/L. Meanwhile, CI-chips suitable for the detection platform are fabricated. Due to the independence of different detection areas on the detection chips, there is no mutual interference or restriction among multiple detection targets.

The detection platform can detect multiple pollutants simultaneously, thereby significantly enhancing the detection efficiency and meeting the global demand for comprehensive detection of water pollutants. Using the digital platform to accumulate data over time, long-term trend analysis of pollutants can be carried out to meet the global planning requirements for sustainable water management. Therefore, it can be widely applied in fields such as water pollutant detection and real-time monitoring of water bodies.

Shichao Guo: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Li Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization. Lin Hu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Formal analysis. Qiangqiang Tao: Writing – review & editing, Software, Methodology. Qiuyan Hu: Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Fu Feng: Validation, Formal analysis. Wei Jin: Software. Chuanbin Li: Data curation, Conceptualization. Ning Hu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial support from the Major Project of Technological Innovation in Hubei Province (Grant No. 2019ACA152), and the Key Research and Development Program of Hubei Province (Grant No. 2021BCD002). We are grateful for the assistance from the National Natural Science Foundation of China Youth Program (Fund number: 52304428).

ce:section.ce:para:
